text.title: Unit Test Anti-Patterns
text.summary: The most damaging unit test anti-patterns: testing implementation details, flaky assertions, excessive mocking, slow tests, and shared mutable state.

antipattern.description: Asserting on private methods or internal state
antipattern.code:
def test_bad(): svc = OrderService() svc.process(order) assert svc._internal_cache["order_123"] == order # tests internal detail
def test_good(): svc = OrderService() result = svc.process(order) assert result.status == "confirmed" # tests observable behavior
antipattern.reason: Tests tied to implementation break on every refactor, even when behavior is correct.

antipattern.description: Asserting on floating-point equality without tolerance
antipattern.code: assert total == 0.1 + 0.2 # fails: 0.30000000000000004 assert abs(total - 0.3) < 1e-9 # correct
antipattern.reason: Floating-point arithmetic is not exact — use pytest.approx() or math.isclose().

antipattern.description: Asserting on dictionary ordering or set iteration order
antipattern.reason: Python dicts are ordered by insertion since 3.7, but set ordering is undefined. Use sorted() for sets, explicit key lookups for dicts.

antipattern.description: Mocking every dependency in a unit test, including simple value objects
antipattern.reason: When the test mocks everything, it proves nothing. Use real collaborators within your own codebase; only mock external boundaries (I/O, time, network).

antipattern.description: Unit test touches the filesystem, database, or network
antipattern.reason: Unit tests must run in milliseconds. Use tmp_path for files, in-memory SQLite for DB, mocks for network. Slow unit tests → developers skip running them locally.

antipattern.description: Tests share a mutable object created once in conftest or setUp
antipattern.reason: Shared mutable state makes tests order-dependent and non-repeatable.
